fuzzy_prd: We need a feature flag engine for our PHP application that lets us roll out new capabilities gradually and toggle them per-entity without touching the codebase for every change. The idea is to register each flag by name with a resolver function that computes its initial value — the resolver can return a constant, check the entity it is being evaluated for, or draw from a probability lottery. Once registered, a flag is considered 'on' unless its value is exactly boolean false; every other value (zero, empty string, null, complex objects) counts as active. Callers can override flags at runtime to force them on or off, and can apply that override to all currently known entity-scope combinations at once. Checking a flag without specifying an entity should automatically use whoever is currently logged in as the entity; and the empty-string entity should be treated as a distinct slot from 'no entity'. Each (flag, entity) pair should be resolved at most once and cached; there should be a way to pre-load values and to discard cached values to force fresh resolution. The engine should also support reading the concrete value of a flag (not just its on/off state), querying multiple flags at once, gating execution on whether a flag is active or inactive, and checking whether middleware should block an HTTP request when required flags are missing. Error cases — like requesting a concrete value for multiple entities simultaneously — should produce machine-readable category codes, consistent with how the auth and scope modules report problems.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: function define(string $feature, callable $resolver): void; function active(string $feature, mixed $scope = null): bool; function value(string $feature, mixed $scope = null): mixed; function activate(string|array $feature, mixed $scope = null, mixed $value = true): void; function deactivate(string|array $feature, mixed $scope = null): void; function forget(string $feature, mixed $scope = null): void; function purge(array|null $features = null): void

trigger_keywords: when is a feature considered off
truthiness rule for feature active check
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A feature is inactive if and only if its resolved value is exactly boolean false (PHP strict false). Every other value is active: true, 0, '', null, [], objects, and any truthy value. The 'inactive' check is the exact logical negation of 'active'.

trigger_keywords: difference between null scope and empty string scope
how is the global scope different from empty string
null vs empty string for unscoped checks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: null scope and no-scope-argument both refer to the global/default scope and are interchangeable — a value stored globally is visible under null and vice versa. The empty string '' is a distinct scope key: values stored under '' are not accessible globally/under null, and global values are not accessible under ''.

trigger_keywords: format of value and all output lines
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Output line formats: 'active <feature> = <true|false>', 'inactive <feature> = <true|false>', 'value <feature> = <json-encoded-value>', 'all = <json-object>', 'calls <feature> = <n>', 'allAreActive = <true|false>', 'someAreActive = <true|false>', 'when <feature> = active <json-value>' or 'when <feature> = inactive', 'defined = <json-array>', 'middleware = blocked status=<n>' or 'middleware = passed'.

trigger_keywords: what does purge with no arguments do
how to reset all feature values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: forget(feature, scope) removes the stored value for one (feature, scope) pair. purge([feature1, feature2]) removes stored values for those features across all scopes. purge() with no arguments (or null) removes stored values for every feature across all scopes. After purging, the next read falls back to the resolver.

trigger_keywords: error code for multiple scope value query
what error when value called with list of scopes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Calling 'value' with a list of scopes (e.g., scope: [1, 2, 3]) is not supported for a single-value query and emits 'error=multiple_scope_values'. An unserializable scope object emits 'error=unserializable_scope'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: consistent with how the auth and scope modules report problems
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The adapter uses neutral 'error=<category>' lines for all error conditions, never exposing PHP exception class names. The loginUser operation sets the default scope resolver to use the specified user ID, making unscoped checks equivalent to checks for that user's scope object.

The 'lottery' resolver type in the adapter: {type: 'lottery', wins: N, out: M} draws a random boolean where wins/out is the probability. When wins==out the result is always true; when wins==0 the result is always false.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under laravel__pennant/golden/src/ for the authoritative specification.

active on = true
active off = false
